query: definition and difference between agenesis, aplasia, atresia, and atrophy

retrieved_docs.title: Posterior Cortical Atrophy -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Posterior cortical atrophy typically presents early (< 65 years). An international study of 302 patients with posterior cortical atrophy showed a peak onset between 50 and 65 years of age with diminishing incidence with age. [7] The mean age of onset was determined to be 58.9 years. A precise estimation of the incidence and prevalence of posterior cortical atrophy is difficult given the changing definitions and the relative rarity of the disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypoplastic Lung Disease -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: The second method for detecting hypoplasia is the radial alveolar count (RAC), which Emery and Mithal initially proposed. The RAC is the number of alveoli crossing a line drawn from a respiratory bronchiole center to the nearest and definitive connective tissue septum. Askenazi and Perlman modified the counting of the alveolar septae instead of the alveoli. They suggested that RAC less than 75% of the mean or LW:BW  less than or equal to 0.012 be used for diagnosing pulmonary hypoplasia. [20] Results from a recent study demonstrated that a diagnosis was established in more cases when the LW:BW ratio and RAC were considered for analysis, with concordance observed in 73.33% of cases. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Holoprosencephaly -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: An MRI is used to diagnose HPE definitively. Depending on the clinical and MRI findings, HPE can be classified into alobar, semilobar, lobar, and middle intrahemispheric variants.

retrieved_docs.title: Tracheoesophageal Fistula -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic factors do not seem to play a significant role in the pathogenesis of TEF. The concordance rate for twins is 2.5%. [6] The incidence of TEF is approximately 1 in 3500 births. [7] [8] Esophageal atresia and TEF are classified according to their anatomic configuration. [9] Type C, which consists of a proximal esophageal pouch and a distal TEF, accounts for 84% of cases. TEF occurs without esophageal atresia (H-type fistula) in only 4% (see Image. Types of Tracheoesophageal Fistulas). [10] Depaepe et al identified a decreasing incidence in the birth frequency of TEF in different European regions. [11] TEF does not correlate with maternal age if chromosomal anomalies are excluded. The figures for isolated TEF versus an association with other congenital anomalies vary between 38.7% and 57.3%. [12] The incidence of trisomies and other chromosomal abnormalities in association with TEF is between 6% and 10%. Neonates younger than 28 weeks of gestation have been excluded. Though significantly rarer, trisomy 18 is associated with TEF more frequently than trisomy 21. Feingold syndrome, manifesting with microcephaly, micrognathia, and digital anomalies, may have an associated TEF or esophageal atresia. In 1962, Waterston et al stratified the risk criteria for patients with TEF based on birth weight, pneumonia, and associated anomalies. [13] Later, Spitz et al proposed a less complicated system based on associated congenital heart defects and low birth weight. [14] Survival in babies less than 1500 g and without significant cardiac anomalies now approaches 97% but falls dramatically to 22% if birth weight is low and cardiac anomalies exist. Acute morbidity and mortality are most commonly due to cardiac and chromosomal defects. Late mortality is due to ongoing respiratory complications. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Cilia Incarnata -- Differential Diagnosis -- Definition
retrieved_docs.content: Hypertrichosis is an increase in hair beyond the normal variation for a patient's age, sex, or ethnicity. Eyelash hypertrichosis is known as trichomegaly.

retrieved_docs.title: Sideroblastic Anemia -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Sideroblastic anemia is considered a rare disease. [7] By definition, rare diseases affect fewer than 200,000 people in the United States. Due to the low incidence and prevalence, researchers do not have definite statistical data on its epidemiology. The disorder affects a wide age range, from infants with congenital forms to middle-aged and older individuals. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: PHACE Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: An interprofessional group of specialists has established the 2009 diagnostic criteria, which had an update in 2016. The diagnostic basis of definite PS is the presence of a facial hemangioma of more than 5 cm with one major criterion or two minor criteria. Possible PS requires the presence of cervicofacial hemangioma with one minor criterion. [11] Major criteria: Arterial: anomalies of major cerebral or cervical arteries, stenosis, occlusion, dysplasia, hypoplasia, persistent carotid-vertebrobasilar anastomosis Structural brain: posterior fossa anomalies: Danky-Walker complex, unilateral or bilateral cerebral dysplasia or hypoplasia Cardiovascular: aortic arch anomalies, aneurysm, an aberrant origin of the subclavian artery Ocular: posterior segment anomaly, retinal vascular anomalies Ventral or midline: sternal defect, sternal cleft Minor criteria:

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Number -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Agenesis of teeth often occurs in association with a syndrome and ranges from the absence of a single tooth to the failure of all the teeth to develop. Milder cases of dental agenesis can typically be restored or addressed by relatively noninvasive measures. Anomalies in the number of teeth are classified as anodontia, hypodontia, oligodontia, or hyperdontia based on the presence or absence of the number of teeth involved.

retrieved_docs.title: Macrocephaly -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: By definition, macrocephaly is found in about 2% to 3% of the population. There is not a significant difference between genders. The specifics of epidemiology, including geographic distribution, depending on the particular underlying cause.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Cerebral Aqueduct (Sylvian) -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Compression of the aqueduct by the vein of Galen or Vein of Galen aneurysmal malformation (VGAM) is a congenital vascular malformation that accounts for 30% of all pediatric vascular abnormalities. The vascular defect causes the shunting of arterial blood from choroidal circulation to the prosencephalic vein of Markowski (MProsV of Markowski). In healthy development, the MProsV of Markowski completely closes off as their role is taken over by the internal cerebral veins. Formation of arteriovenous shunts connecting the choroidal circulation with the MProsV of Markowski cause increase flow through the MProsV of Markowski and prevent its closure. The continued patency of the MProsV of Markowski is known as a VGAM. Hydrocephalus and congestive heart failure are both presenting features of VGAM. Both a decrease absorption of CSF as well as a compression of the cerebral aqueduct can cause the clinical picture of hydrocephalus. In infants, a trans-fontanelle ultrasound can be used to evaluate the brain anatomy and identify a VGAM. MR imaging can help make a definitive diagnosis and assess comorbid conditions such as brain infractions, atrophy, and hydrocephalus. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Poland Syndrome in Children -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The only defining criterion in the diversity and variation of Poland syndrome is pectoral muscle deficiency. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Beevor Sign -- Definition/Introduction -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: FSHD is an autosomal dominant muscular dystrophy in which the Beevor sign is regarded as a sine qua non clinical feature. [8] While some studies suggest that the sign has 90% sensitivity and specificity for FSHD, others argue that it is highly specific (over 90%) but less sensitive (54%) and may aid in diagnosis. [9] Additionally, the Beevor sign is more commonly found in typical than atypical cases of FSHD. Less frequent reports of this sign have been documented in the following diseases:

retrieved_docs.title: Constitutional Growth Delay -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The disease of constitutional growth delay is a diagnosis of exclusion. Therefore the proper way to assess this medical diagnosis is by short stature, which by definition affects about 3% of the population — defined as a growth velocity below 5cm a year (in the childhood age). This linear velocity change depends on the age group, that’s why it is also necessary to assess short stature and growth delay at different stages in life as it presents differently in a toddler (6  to 7cm), vs. childhood (5.5cm) and adolescence (girls 8 to 12cm boys 10 to 14cm). [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Hirschsprung Disease -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The absence of the argyrophilic plexus, an infrequent differential diagnosis of HD, might be caused by the disturbed differentiation of the argyrophilic cells. [44] Megacystis microcolon intestinal hypoperistalsis syndrome (MMIHS) is categorized as one of the least common disorders in this spectrum. It might cause the most severe clinical presentations of neonatal functional obstruction syndromes. Several hypotheses have been proposed to identify the exact mechanistic responsible pathways. Although the definitive description has not been well established yet, mutations in the nicotinic acetylcholine receptor (nAChR) and a lack of expression of the alpha3, beta2, and beta4 subunits of nAChR in small bowel tissue might have similar clinical manifestations. [45] [46] [47]

retrieved_docs.title: Albinism -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Aniridia: The absence of the iris, which demonstrates foveal hypoplasia, nystagmus, amblyopia, and cataracts

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2818
retrieved_docs.content: Conditions of fetal growth and malnutrition are regarded as a parent category that includes the specific outcomes of short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. The grouping of short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation serves as a classification label for these adverse perinatal conditions, emphasizing their interrelated nature in the context of impaired fetal development and inadequate nutrition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized as a related group of adverse perinatal outcomes and are classified together as a distinct clinical category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 547
retrieved_docs.content: The entity “CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION” is defined as a parent category that includes the combined condition “short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation.” This combined condition is used as both a descriptor and a classification label for itself, emphasizing its role as a distinct clinical grouping. The specific diagnosis “Foetale ondervoeding zonder vermelding van licht of klein voor zwangerschapsduur” (fetal undernutrition without reference to being light or small for gestational age) is classified under the “short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation” grouping, indicating that it shares the same underlying features of premature delivery, reduced birth weight, and impaired fetal growth. In addition, “Foetale ondervoeding zonder vermelding van licht of klein voor zwangerschapsduur” has a broader relationship to the Arabic‑language concept “اضطراب لدى الأطفال وحديثي الولادة,” which denotes a disorder affecting infants and newborns. Together, these relationships illustrate how fetal growth restriction, prematurity, and low birth weight are interconnected components of fetal malnutrition and form part of a larger spectrum of neonatal disorders, underscoring the importance of recognizing these linked conditions for comprehensive perinatal assessment and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1498
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION encompass the specific subgroup identified as short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation. This subgroup is both defined by and classified under the term تقييد النمو الجنيني (fetal growth restriction). تقييد النمو الجنيني, in turn, is linked to a broader, unspecified condition that arises in the perinatal period, described as حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة. Together, these relationships illustrate that short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized components of fetal growth restriction, which itself forms part of a larger, not‑yet‑specified perinatal disorder.

retrieved_docs.content: (unspecified condition originating in the perinatal period), situating fetal growth restriction within a larger, undefined perinatal condition category. Together, these relationships illustrate how gestational duration, low birth weight, fetal growth restriction, and associated perinatal disorders are organized within medical classification systems, aiding clinicians in diagnosing and managing newborns with short gestation and related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2270
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical presentation described as “Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation” is used to define and classify that same set of perinatal abnormalities, and it is categorized under the term “تقييد النمو الجنيني,” which translates to fetal growth restriction. This grouping emphasizes that short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are collectively recognized as a single entity of fetal growth restriction in medical classification.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 173
retrieved_docs.content: Certain conditions originating in the perinatal period (P00‑P96) constitute a broad diagnostic category that includes, as a child entity, Respiratory and cardiovascular disorders specific to the perinatal period (P19‑P29). Within the same overarching group, the condition Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation is linked as a parent concept, indicating that it is a more specific manifestation of perinatal disorders. This condition is classified both as تقييد النمو الجنيني (fetal growth restriction) and as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن} (extremely low birth weight, unspecified), reflecting its dual characterization in terms of growth limitation and birth‑weight severity. The concept تقييد النمو الجنيني has a broader relationship to حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة (unspecified condition originating in the perinatal period), suggesting that fetal growth restriction is part of a larger, less defined set of perinatal abnormalities. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2901
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation is recognized as a specific perinatal condition that is classified as تقييد النمو الجنيني, indicating fetal growth restriction, and also as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}, denoting a birth at the highest degree of prematurity with an unspecified weight. This dual classification underscores the clinical importance of identifying both the limited fetal growth and the extreme prematurity associated with these presentations for appropriate medical management and follow‑up.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: The medical framework links gestational age, birth outcomes, and perinatal disorders through a hierarchical classification system. A gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a precursor to a liveborn outcome, indicating that infants born after this duration can survive outside the womb. Duration of gestation is positioned as a broader category that inversely encompasses liveborn, underscoring that the length of pregnancy defines the viability of a live birth. Conditions associated with short gestation and low birth weight — collectively described as Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation — serve as a primary classifier for liveborn infants, reflecting the clinical importance of prematurity and insufficient fetal growth in determining neonatal status. This same condition is also classified as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن} (“birth at the highest degree of prematurity, unspecified weight”), reinforcing its role as a marker of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 180
retrieved_docs.content: also underlies dyspareunia (painful sexual intercourse), which is classified under the same “Other specified female genital disorders” concept. In the pediatric context, infants born preterm with low birth weight have an increased risk of short stature (قصر القامة), and short stature itself is associated with higher rates of neonatal death. Unspecified obstetric codes for care or intervention encompass unspecified malposition or malpresentation, reflecting the hierarchical relationship between broad labor‑delivery indications and specific obstetric complications. Together, these interrelated entities illustrate how structural cervical weakness, congenital pelvic anomalies, prior surgical scars, and associated fetal‑maternal outcomes converge to inform risk assessment, diagnosis, and targeted management in maternal‑fetal medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 151
retrieved_docs.content: The concepts describe a hierarchical classification of perinatal and neonatal conditions centered on gestational age and birth outcomes. A gestational period of **29‑30 weeks of gestation completed** is linked to the outcome of a **liveborn** infant, indicating that births occurring at this gestational age are considered liveborn. The broader category **Duration of gestation** is inversely related to **liveborn**, reflecting that liveborn status is defined by the length of gestation. The condition **Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation** serves as a classifier for **liveborn** births and is itself classified as **تقييد النمو الجنيني** (fetal growth restriction). This condition also functions as a parent category for **Certain conditions originating in the perinatal period (P00‑P96)**, which in turn includes **Respiratory and cardiovascular disorders specific to the perinatal period (P19‑P29)** as a child. **CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION** is

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2019
retrieved_docs.content: The Clinical Data Interchange Standards Consortium Terminology defines a specific subset that includes concepts related to digestive congenital anomalies, indicating that within the CDISC framework these anomalies are organized as a distinct category to support standardized representation of associated clinical concepts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2873
retrieved_docs.content: CDISC SEND Terminology includes digestive congenital anomalies as a subset concept within its classification system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 102
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of a liveborn infant encompasses a range of perinatal classifications that are linked through gestational age, birthweight, and fetal growth status. A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a specific child category of liveborn, indicating that infants born alive at this stage are included within the broader liveborn group. The duration of gestation has an inverse‑isa relationship with liveborn, reflecting that shorter gestational periods are associated with distinct clinical classifications of liveborn infants. One major classification of liveborn infants is short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation. This category both classifies liveborn and is self‑referential, emphasizing its central role in describing infants born with reduced gestational length, low birth weight, or impaired growth. The disorder relating to short gestation AND/OR low birthweight is positioned as a parent of liveborn, indicating that this disorder underlies the

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2763
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation constitute a severe perinatal condition that is classified as an extremely premature birth of the highest degree with unspecified weight (ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}). This classification highlights that the infant is born far before term, exhibits markedly reduced body weight, and shows evidence of impaired intra‑uterine growth, all of which place the newborn at high risk for morbidity and mortality and necessitate intensive neonatal monitoring and intervention.
